skill.name: accessibility-wcag
skill.description: Guide UI creation and review against WCAG 2.2 accessibility standards, including semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard access, focus handling, form labels, touch targets, and color contrast.
skill.summary: Review and improve frontend accessibility using WCAG 2.2 checks.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, Korean text, CSS examples, and reference links. The only confirmed risk is low-severity command guidance in a bash example that suggests npm installs and running Lighthouse CI.

low_findings.title: Package Installation Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill includes a bash example that suggests installing axe-core, eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y, and Lighthouse CI. These commands are normal for accessibility testing, but users should review package sources and project policy before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a documentation code block and have a legitimate accessibility testing purpose. The risk is low because there is no hidden execution path or exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Reference URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to WCAG 2.2, WAI-ARIA 1.2, and eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y references. These are documentation links, not automated network calls or data transfer endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in the References section and point to public standards or tool documentation. No evidence found of network requests, credential transfer, or remote code loading.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Analyzer False Positives from Markdown Fences
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown code fence delimiters around TSX, CSS, JSON, and checklist examples. These are content examples rather than executable Ruby or shell backticks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows fenced Markdown examples, not Ruby code execution. The only real shell content is the explicit bash example for testing tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed High-Severity Heuristic Findings
dangerous_patterns.description: The certificate/key, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, entropy, and dangerous-combination alerts are not supported by the file content. The matched lines contain frontmatter, CSS, keyboard event examples, Unicode text, and reference links.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no credential files, cryptographic implementation, reconnaissance command, obfuscated payload, or combined execution and exfiltration logic. The entropy signal is plausibly caused by mixed Korean and Markdown content.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss accessibility issues until late review or user complaints. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a WCAG 2.2 checklist for building and reviewing accessible UI.
actual_capabilities: Checks UI code for semantic HTML and appropriate heading structure.
Reviews images, forms, and controls for alt text, labels, and ARIA usage.
Identifies keyboard navigation, focus visibility, and skip-link gaps.
Applies WCAG 2.2 AA guidance for target size, dragging alternatives, and obscured focus.
Suggests automated checks with axe-core, eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y, and Lighthouse CI.
limitations: Does not run accessibility tools unless the assistant is allowed to execute commands.
Cannot replace testing with real screen readers and keyboard-only workflows.
Does not verify legal compliance for a specific country or industry.
Color contrast values may need confirmation with a dedicated contrast checker.

use_cases.title: Review a React component
use_cases.description: Check a component for semantic markup, labels, ARIA attributes, focus behavior, and keyboard access before merge.

use_cases.title: Prepare a design-system control
use_cases.description: Validate buttons, dialogs, form fields, and drag interactions against WCAG 2.2 AA expectations.

use_cases.title: Audit a release candidate
use_cases.description: Create a practical accessibility checklist for manual review and automated tooling before launch.

prompt_templates.title: Basic accessibility review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for one UI component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component for WCAG 2.2 accessibility issues. Focus on semantic HTML, labels, alt text, keyboard access, and focus visibility.

prompt_templates.title: Fix identified issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this UI to meet WCAG 2.2 AA guidance. Explain each accessibility change and avoid changing unrelated behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a test checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an accessibility test checklist for this page. Include keyboard-only checks, screen reader checks, contrast checks, and automated tool suggestions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit complex interaction
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of dialogs, drag actions, or custom controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this interaction against WCAG 2.2 AA. Include focus management, ARIA state, target size, dragging alternatives, and failure risks.

output_examples.input: Review a form with placeholder-only email and password inputs.
output: Add visible labels linked to each input.
Keep placeholder text as helper text only.
Ensure validation errors use clear text and an accessible announcement pattern.

output_examples.input: Review a clickable card implemented with a generic container.
output: Use a native link or button when the whole card is interactive.
Provide keyboard focus and activation support.
Keep a visible focus style that meets contrast requirements.

output_examples.input: Review a draggable list used for task ordering.
output: Keep drag support, but add move up and move down buttons.
Announce reorder results for assistive technology users.
Make each control at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels when practical.

best_practices: Start with native HTML controls before adding ARIA.
Test every interactive element with keyboard-only navigation.
Combine automated checks with manual review and assistive technology testing.
anti_patterns: Do not remove focus outlines without adding a visible replacement.
Do not use color alone to communicate errors, state, or priority.
Do not add ARIA roles that conflict with native element semantics.

faq.question: Which WCAG version does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets WCAG 2.2, with practical emphasis on AA-level frontend checks.

faq.question: Can it automatically prove a page is compliant?
faq.answer: No. It can guide reviews and fixes, but compliance needs testing and organizational signoff.

faq.question: Does it support React?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples focus on TSX and React-style components, but the principles apply broadly.

faq.question: Does it require running commands?
faq.answer: No. Command examples are optional suggestions for automated accessibility tools.

faq.question: Can it review ARIA usage?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks roles, labels, live regions, modal attributes, and loading states.

faq.question: Is this a legal accessibility audit?
faq.answer: No. It is technical guidance and should not replace legal review.
